title: On the Chvátal-Janson Conjecture
overall_summary: The paper proves, for every integer n≥2, the Chvátal-Janson statement about the sequence q_m=P(B(n,m/n)≤m), where B(n,m/n) is binomial with n trials and success probability m/n: q_m is minimized at the nearest integer to 2n/3, and the adjacent monotonicity is exactly q_m≥q_{m+1} iff m+1/2<2n/3. The proof represents q_m through uniform order statistics and beta integrals, reduces adjacent comparisons q_m≥q_{m+1} to a normalized one-dimensional integral inequality h_n(m)≥1, proves h_n(m) is decreasing in m, and then checks only the boundary cases according to n mod 3. The boundary estimates are elementary but sharp: lower bounds use Bernoulli's inequality and geometric-series bounds, while upper bounds use logarithmic series expansions with cancellation of linear terms, truncation after cubic terms, exp(c)<1+c+c^2/2 for c<0, and polynomial integration. For the original weighted Bernoulli problem, the paper is most directly useful for equal-weight configurations, where probabilities reduce to binomial tails at integer means; it supplies exact finite-n control of how those tails vary with the grid parameter m/n.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Main theorem proved by the paper, all-n Chvátal-Janson monotonicity
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let n≥2 be an integer. For each m∈{0,1,...,n}, let B(n,m/n) denote a binomial random variable with n trials and success probability m/n, and define q_m=P(B(n,m/n)≤m). Then, for every m∈{0,1,...,n−1}, q_m≥q_{m+1} if and only if m+1/2<2n/3. Consequently the sequence q_0,...,q_n attains its minimum at m=[[2n/3]], where [[x]] denotes the nearest integer to x.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof starts from the order-statistic representation q_m=P(U_(m+1)>m/n), where U_(j) is the j-th order statistic of n independent Uniform[0,1] variables. This gives a beta-integral formula for q_m. Lemma 1 compares the beta-integral formulas for q_m and q_{m+1}; after integration by parts and changes of variables, for 1≤m≤n−2 the comparison q_m≥q_{m+1} becomes h_n(m)≥1, where h_n(m)=∫_0^1 (1−v/(m+1))^m (1+v/(n−m−1))^(n−m−1) dv. Lemma 2 proves that h_n(m) is decreasing in m, by differentiating the logarithm of the integrand with respect to real m and using elementary logarithmic inequalities. Thus, for fixed n, it is enough to check h_n(m) on the two indices around the predicted cutoff. Write n=3s+r with r∈{0,1,2}. If r=0, the cutoff is between m=2s−1 and m=2s, so it suffices to prove h_{3s}(2s−1)≥1 and h_{3s}(2s)<1. If r=1 or r=2, the cutoff is between m=2s and m=2s+1, so it suffices to prove h_{3s+r}(2s)≥1 and h_{3s+r}(2s+1)<1. These are exactly the boundary integral estimates displayed as equations (8), (9), and (10) in the paper. The estimates are proved by elementary inequalities: Bernoulli's inequality gives the lower-bound sides, while logarithmic series truncation and polynomial upper bounds give the upper-bound sides. Endpoint comparisons not covered by the h_n formula are direct: q_0=1, q_n=1, and q_{n−1}<q_n for n≥2.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Equations (1)-(2), order-statistic and beta-integral representation of q_m
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let n≥2, let U_1,...,U_n be independent Uniform[0,1] random variables, and let U_(1)≤...≤U_(n) be their order statistics. For m∈{0,...,n−1}, define q_m=P(B(n,m/n)≤m). Then q_m=P(Σ_{i=1}^n 1_{U_i≤m/n}≤m)=P(U_(m+1)>m/n), and hence q_m=(m+1) binom(n,m+1) ∫_{m/n}^1 x^m(1−x)^(n−m−1) dx.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Couple B(n,m/n) as the number of uniforms U_i that fall in [0,m/n]. The event that at most m uniforms are at most m/n is equivalent to the event that the (m+1)-st order statistic exceeds m/n. The density of U_(m+1) is f_{m+1}(x)=(m+1) binom(n,m+1) x^m(1−x)^(n−m−1) on [0,1]. Integrating this density over x∈(m/n,1] yields the displayed formula.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 1, equivalent integral criteria for adjacent comparisons
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let n≥2 and let m be an integer with 0≤m≤n−2. With q_m=P(B(n,m/n)≤m), define b_m=((m/n)^(m+1)(1−m/n)^(n−m−1))/(n−m−1). Then q_m≥q_{m+1} if and only if ∫_{m/n}^{(m+1)/n} x^(m+1)(1−x)^(n−m−2) dx ≥ b_m. In addition, for the displayed formulas with 1/m, i.e. for m≥1, this comparison is equivalent to ∫_0^1 (1+t/m)^(m+1)(1−t/(n−m))^(n−m−2) dt ≥ (n−m)/(n−m−1), and also equivalent to ∫_0^1 (1−v/(m+1))^m (1+v/(n−m−1))^(n−m−1) dv ≥ 1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Insert the beta-integral representation of q_m and q_{m+1}. After simplifying the binomial coefficients, q_m≥q_{m+1} is equivalent to ((m+1)/(n−m−1)) ∫_{m/n}^1 x^m(1−x)^(n−m−1) dx ≥ ∫_{(m+1)/n}^1 x^(m+1)(1−x)^(n−m−2) dx. Integrating by parts in the left-hand integral transforms the left side into −b_m+∫_{m/n}^1 x^(m+1)(1−x)^(n−m−2) dx, so cancellation with the right side leaves precisely the integral over [m/n,(m+1)/n] bounded below by b_m. For the second form, substitute x=m/n+t/n in that interval and divide by (m/n)^(m+1)(1−m/n)^(n−m−2). For the third form, multiply the second inequality by (n−m−1)/(n−m), integrate by parts on the resulting left-hand side, divide by the endpoint factor (1−1/(n−m))^(n−m−1)(1+1/m)^m, and finally set t=1−v.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Lemma 2, monotonicity of the normalized comparison integral
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix n≥3. For each v∈(0,1], define, for real x∈[1,n−2], g_v(x)=(1−v/(x+1))^x (1+v/(n−x−1))^(n−x−1). Then g_v is decreasing on [1,n−2]. Consequently h(x)=∫_0^1 (1−v/(x+1))^x (1+v/(n−x−1))^(n−x−1) dv is decreasing on [1,n−2].
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Differentiate log g_v(x). The derivative has the sign of log(1−v/(x+1)) + [v x/(x+1)^2]/[1−v/(x+1)] − log(1+v/(n−x−1)) + [v/(n−x−1)]/[1+v/(n−x−1)]. Thus g_v'(x)<0 follows if [v/(n−x−1)]/[1+v/(n−x−1)] < log(1+v/(n−x−1)) and log(1+ (v/(x+1))/(1−v/(x+1))) > [v x/(x+1)^2]/[1−v/(x+1)]. The first inequality is the elementary log inequality c/(1+c)<log(1+c) for c>0. For the second, set c=v/(x+1−v); after algebra it becomes log(1+c)−c+c^2/[v(c+1)]>0. Since v≤1, this left side is at least log(1+c)−c+c^2/(c+1)=log(1+c)−c/(c+1), which is positive again by c/(1+c)<log(1+c). Therefore the derivative is strictly negative for v∈(0,1], and integrating over v gives monotonicity of h.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Boundary estimates, equations (8)-(10)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let n=3s+r with s≥1 and r∈{0,1,2}. The proof uses the following boundary inequalities for the decreasing integral h_n(m)=∫_0^1 (1−v/(m+1))^m (1+v/(n−m−1))^(n−m−1) dv. For r=0, h_{3s}(2s−1)=∫_0^1 (1−v/(2s))^(2s−1)(1+v/s)^s dv ≥1, and for s≥2, h_{3s}(2s)=∫_0^1 (1−v/(2s+1))^(2s)(1+v/(s−1))^(s−1) dv <1. For r=1,2, h_{3s+r}(2s)=J_r:=∫_0^1 (1−v/(2s+1))^(2s)(1+v/(s+r−1))^(s+r−1) dv ≥1. Also, for the valid denominator ranges, h_{3s+r}(2s+1)=H_r:=∫_0^1 (1−v/(2s+2))^(2s+1)(1+v/(s+r−2))^(s+r−2) dv <1; the proof reduces this to H_2<1, and H_1≤H_2 when H_1 is meaningful. Small endpoint cases where a displayed denominator would be zero are handled directly in the proof of the theorem.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For the lower bound h_{3s}(2s−1)≥1, rewrite the integrand as [(1−v/(2s))^2(1+v/s)]^s (1−v/(2s))^(−1). The bracket equals 1−3v^2/(4s^2)+v^3/(4s^3). Bernoulli's inequality (1+c)^s≥1+sc gives a lower bound by (1−3v^2/(4s)+v^3/(4s^2))(1−v/(2s))^(−1). Using (1−c)^(−1)>1+c+c^2 for c∈(0,1), integration gives 1+5/(96s^2)−1/(80s^3)+1/(96s^4)>1. For the upper bound h_{3s}(2s)<1, define I_s=∫_0^1 (1−v/(2s+1))^(2s)(1+v/(s−1))^(s−1) dv. Write I_s=∫_0^1 [(2s+1)/(2s+1−v)] exp((2s+1)log(1−v/(2s+1))+(s−1)log(1+v/(s−1))) dv. The linear terms in the logarithmic series cancel, giving an exponent Σ_{k≥2} v^k/k [(-1)^(k−1)/(s−1)^(k−1) − 1/(2s+1)^(k−1)]. The tail from k=4 onward is negative, so the exponent is bounded above by γ(s,v), the k=2,3 truncation: γ(s,v)=−3v^2 s/[2(s−1)(2s+1)] + v^3 s(s+2)/[(s−1)^2(2s+1)^2], and γ(s,v)<0 for s≥2 and v∈(0,1]. Then exp(γ)<1+γ+γ^2/2, and (2s+1)/(2s+1−v)≤1+v/(2s+1)+5v^2/[4(2s+1)^2]. Integrating the resulting polynomial gives I_s < 1 + [1+3s(−11s^3+(s+4)^2)]/[(s−1)^4(2s+1)^6] + [s^6(29+7s−15s^2/2)]/[(s−1)^4(2s+1)^6]. Both displayed numerators are negative for s≥3, and I_2<1 is checked by direct computation. For J_r, the inequality (1+v/s)^s≤(1+v/(s+1))^(s+1) implies J_1≤J_2, so it suffices to prove J_1≥1. Rewrite J_1 as ∫_0^1 [(1−v/(2s+1))^2(1+v/s)]^s dv, expand the bracket as 1+v(1−2v)/[s(2s+1)] + [v^2/(2s+1)^2](1+v/s), and apply Bernoulli's inequality. Integration gives J_1≥1−1/[6(2s+1)] + s/[3(2s+1)^2] + 1/[4(2s+1)^2] = 1+1/[12(2s+1)^2]. For H_r, the inequality (1+v/(s−1))^(s−1)≤(1+v/s)^s gives H_1≤H_2, so it suffices to show H_2<1. The proof repeats the logarithmic-series method: H_2=∫_0^1 [(2s+2)/(2s+2−v)] exp((2s+2)log(1−v/(2s+2))+s log(1+v/s)) dv. The exponent has zero linear term and is bounded above by its k=2,3 truncation λ(s,v)=Σ_{k=2}^3 v^k/k [(-1)^(k−1)/s^(k−1) − 1/(2s+2)^(k−1)], with λ(s,v)<0 for s≥2. Use exp(λ)<1+λ+λ^2/2 and (2s+2)/(2s+2−v)<1+v/(2s+2)+6v^2/[5(2s+2)^2]. Polynomial integration yields H_2 < 1 + [−2s^5−9(s^4+s^3)+8s^2+22s+18]/[64s(s+1)^6], which is less than 1 for s≥2; H_2<1 for s=1 is checked directly.

proof_techniques.name: Uniform order-statistic encoding of binomial tails
proof_techniques.description: The paper realizes B(n,m/n) as the count of independent Uniform[0,1] variables below the threshold m/n. The lower tail event B(n,m/n)≤m is then exactly the order-statistic event U_(m+1)>m/n. This converts the binomial CDF at its mean into a beta-density tail integral with explicit density (m+1)binom(n,m+1)x^m(1−x)^(n−m−1).
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For equal weights in the original weighted Bernoulli problem, the weighted sum is a normalized binomial variable. This representation can convert equal-weight probability inequalities into beta-integral inequalities where calculus and integration by parts can be applied.

proof_techniques.name: Adjacent-comparison normalization by integration by parts
proof_techniques.description: Instead of estimating q_m directly, the paper compares adjacent probabilities q_m and q_{m+1}. After substituting the beta-integral expressions and simplifying binomial coefficients, an integration by parts collapses the comparison to an integral over the short interval [m/n,(m+1)/n] plus a boundary term. Two changes of variables then normalize the comparison into the clean criterion h_n(m)≥1, where h_n(m)=∫_0^1 (1−v/(m+1))^m(1+v/(n−m−1))^(n−m−1) dv.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the original problem is attacked first on rational grid values p=m/n and equal weights, adjacent-comparison formulas may reveal where the worst p values lie without needing absolute tail estimates. Similar normalization may also help compare candidate extremal weight vectors.

proof_techniques.name: Treating the discrete index as continuous to prove monotonicity
proof_techniques.description: The paper proves that the normalized comparison integral h_n(m) decreases with m by extending the integrand to real x and differentiating log g_v(x). The derivative is shown negative pointwise in v using only log(1+c)>c/(1+c) and a related inequality obtained after the substitution c=v/(x+1−v). Pointwise monotonicity of the integrand yields monotonicity of the integral.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This technique can reduce a family of finitely many binomial-tail inequalities to checking boundary indices. In the original problem, a comparable continuous-index monotonicity argument might identify extremal equal-weight counterexamples or certify intervals of p.

proof_techniques.name: Boundary reduction by n modulo 3
proof_techniques.description: Once h_n(m) is known to be decreasing, the entire Chvátal-Janson monotonicity pattern follows from two boundary checks. Writing n=3s+r, the cutoff m+1/2<2n/3 translates into checking h_{3s}(2s−1)≥1 and h_{3s}(2s)<1 for r=0, and checking h_{3s+r}(2s)≥1 and h_{3s+r}(2s+1)<1 for r=1,2. This modular decomposition is what turns a global sequence problem into a few explicit integral estimates.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For equal weights and rational p, failures or sharp cases may occur at arithmetic boundary locations depending on n mod a small denominator. This paper's n mod 3 cutoff around 2n/3 is a concrete pattern to test against candidate p thresholds.

proof_techniques.name: Bernoulli-inequality lower bounds after algebraic regrouping
proof_techniques.description: For boundary integrals that must be shown at least 1, the paper groups the integrand into powers of a single bracket, such as [(1−v/(2s))^2(1+v/s)]^s, expands the bracket exactly, and applies Bernoulli's inequality (1+c)^s≥1+sc. Remaining factors like (1−v/(2s))^(−1) are bounded below by a short geometric expansion, and the resulting polynomial is integrated exactly.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This gives a template for rigorous finite-n lower bounds on binomial probabilities without asymptotics. It may be useful when trying to prove the original inequality for ranges of p where equal-weight binomial tails are expected to be safely above the target.

proof_techniques.name: Log-series truncation with negative-tail control for upper bounds
proof_techniques.description: For boundary integrals that must be shown below 1, the paper rewrites the product integrand as a simple rational prefactor times an exponential of logarithms. The logarithmic series has cancellation of the linear term, and the tail from degree 4 onward is negative, so the exponent is bounded above by the quadratic-cubic truncation. That truncation is itself negative; the paper then uses exp(c)<1+c+c^2/2 for c<0, bounds the rational prefactor by a quadratic polynomial, and integrates explicitly to get a rational expression less than 1.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is a robust finite-sample method for proving strict upper bounds on binomial tail integrals near a delicate threshold. It may help establish equal-weight counterexamples or sharp non-validity ranges in the original problem without relying on normal approximations.

proof_techniques.name: Using monotonicity of a↦(1+v/a)^a to compare residue classes
proof_techniques.description: The proof uses simple comparisons such as (1+v/s)^s≤(1+v/(s+1))^(s+1) and (1+v/(s−1))^(s−1)≤(1+v/s)^s for v≥0. These reduce some boundary estimates for r=1,2 to a single harder case, e.g. J_1≥1 implies J_2≥1 and H_2<1 implies H_1<1.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: When equal-weight binomial expressions differ only by neighboring denominator/exponent parameters, this monotonicity can reduce the number of cases needed in a p-classification.

other_useful_info: The paper notes that Janson (2021) had proved the Chvátal conjecture for large n using an Edgeworth expansion for integer-valued central limit probabilities, while this paper gives an elementary all-n proof. It also points out that Rigollet and Tong (2011, Appendix B) used a similar approach to show q_m≥q_{m+1} for 0≤m<n/2 and n≥2. Related references cited for probabilities that a binomial variable exceeds or is at most its expectation include Doerr (2018), Greenberg and Mohri (2014), Jogdeo and Samuels (1968), Pelekis and Ramon (2016), Slud (1977), and Vapnik (1998).
why_relevant: Proves the Chvátal-Janson conjecture for binomial variables with parameter m/n, giving precise control of tails exactly at integer means. This is important for determining which p fail already for equal weights.
